original_abstract: High-resolution X-ray Computed Tomography (HRXCT) or micro-CT (µCT) is a frequently used non-destructive 3D imaging and analysis technique for the investigation of internal structures of a large variety of objects, including geomaterials. Although the possibilities of X-ray micro-CT are becoming better appreciated in earth science research, the demands on this technique are also approaching certain physical limitations. As such, there remains a lot of research to be done in order to solve all the technical problems that occur when higher demands are put on the technique. In this paper, a review of the principle, the advantages and limitations of X-ray CT itself are presented, together with an overview of some current applications of micro-CT in geosciences. One of the main advantages of this technique is the fact that it is a non-destructive characterization technique which allows 4D monitoring of internal structural changes at resolutions down to a few hundred nanometers. Limitations of this technique are the operator dependency for the 3D image analysis from the reconstructed data, the discretations effects and possible imaging artefacts. Driven by the technological and computational progress, the technique is continuously growing as an analysis tool in geosciences and is becoming one of the standard techniques, as is shown by the large and still increasing number of publications in this research area. It is foreseen that this number will continue to rise, and micro-CT will become an indispensable technique in the field of geosciences.
humanized_abstract: High-Resolution X-ray Computed Tomography (HRXCT) or micro-CT (µCT) is one of the most popular non-destructive 3D imaging and analysis methods for investigating the internal structure of wide range of objects from sediments to artefacts and even fossils including geomaterials. While the application of X-ray micro-CT is rapidly increasing in earth science research, the demands on this technique are reaching certain physical and/or practical limits, and therefore there is ample scope for further research into the various technical problems that are emerging when the performance of the technique is pushed to the limits. In this review paper, we summarize the principle, advantages and limitations of X-ray CT and provide an overview of its current applications in the geosciences. The main advantages of this technique are that X-ray micro-CT is a non-destructive characterization technique that allows 4D imaging of the internal structural evolution at the micrometer-scale down to a few hundred nanometers. Some of the disadvantages of this technique include operator dependency in image analysis and discretization effects and possible artefacts. However, the progress in technical and computational aspects are continually improving the applicability of this technique in geosciences, which is indicated by the rapidly growing number of publications in this field. This trend is expected to continue and therefore the micro-CT is expected to become one of the important tools in geosciences.
